title: Case on Impeachment Trial of Commissioner General of National Police Agency
text:
1Case on Impeachment Trial of Commissioner General of National Police Agency
[2024Hun-Na7, December 18, 2025]
* First Draft
In this case, the Court rendered a decision removing Respondent Cho Ji-ho, Commissioner 
General of the National Police Agency, from office on the grounds that he committed grave 
violations of the Constitution and statutes by blocking the National Assembly and controlling 
access thereto during the state of emergency martial law on December 3, 2024 and deploying 
police forces to the Gwacheon headquarters of the National Election Commission and to the 
Suwon Election Training Institute during the same martial law period.
Background of the Case
President Yoon Suk-yeol (hereinafter, unless otherwise specified, the title refers to the 
position held at the time of each act) declared emergency martial law through a public 
address to the nation at around 22:27 on December 3, 2024 (hereinafter, the emergency 
martial law declared on December 3, 2024 is referred to as the “Martial Law”). The address 
stated that the Republic of Korea was in a state of paralysis due to the opposition party’s 
actions such as impeachment, special prosecution, and budget cuts, and that emergency 
martial law was declared in order to defend the liberal Republic of Korea from the threat of 
North Korean communist forces and to protect the constitutional order. President Yoon 
Suk-yeol appointed Park Ansu, Chief of Staff of the Army, as the Martial Law Commander, 
and Park Ansu issued Martial Law Command Proclamation No. 1 (hereinafter referred to as 
the “Proclamation”) at around 23:23 on the same day.
At approximately 01:02 on December 4, 2024, a resolution demanding the lifting of the 
Martial Law was adopted with 190 votes in favor out of 190 members present during the 15th 
plenary of the 418th session (regular) of the National Assembly. At around 04:20 on the same 
day, President Yoon Suk-yeol delivered a public address announcing the end of the Martial 
Law, and at approximately 04:29, the State Council approved the resolution to lift the Martial 
Law.
On December 10, 2024, Kim Yong-min and 169 other members of the National Assembly 
proposed an impeachment resolution against Respondent, Commissioner General of the 
2National Police Agency, on the ground that Respondent violated the Constitution and statutes 
in the performance of official duties by, inter alia, participating in President Yoon Suk-yeol’s 
act of insurrection.
Subject Matter of Review
The subject matter of review in this case is whether Commissioner General of the National 
Police Agency Cho Ji-ho violated the Constitution or statutes in the course of performing his 
official duties and whether a decision to remove him from office should be rendered.
Summary of the Decision
1. Whether Respondent violated the Constitution or statutes in the performance of 
official duties
A. Assessment of the blockade of and access control to the National Assembly during the 
Martial Law
Prior to the declaration of the Martial Law, at the President’s safe house, Respondent heard 
from President Yoon Suk-yeol, together with remarks taking issue with the activities of the 
National Assembly, such as pursuing impeachment and special prosecutor investigations and 
cutting budgets, that emergency martial law would be declared and that soldiers would be 
deployed to various locations, including the National Assembly, and, given that deployment, 
that the police ensure effective control of the National Assembly, and further received from 
Minister of National Defense Kim Yong-hyun a document indicating the time and location of 
troop deployment.
After the Martial Law was declared, Respondent and Kim Bong-sik, Commissioner 
General of the Seoul Metropolitan Police Agency, deployed approximately 300 police 
officers primarily around the entrances to the National Assembly, and, pursuant to 
Respondent’s instructions, access to the National Assembly was completely blocked from 
around 22:48 on December 3, 2024. Thereafter, upon confirming that the Constitution grants 
the National Assembly, inter alia, the authority to request the lifting of martial law, 
Respondent allowed persons with regular access to the National Assembly to enter.
However, when Martial Law Commander Park Ansu issued Martial Law Command 
3Proclamation No. 1 on December 3, 2024 (hereinafter referred to as the “Proclamation”), 
Respondent instructed that access to the National Assembly be completely blocked in 
accordance with the Proclamation, and, as a result, access to the National Assembly was 
again completely blocked from around 23:37 on the same day until around 01:45 on 
December 4, 2024. Consequently, some members of the National Assembly who were 
gathering at the Assembly in order to pass a resolution demanding the lifting of the Martial 
Law had to climb over the fence or were unable to enter the National Assembly at all, and the 
plenary session of the National Assembly was delayed.
Despite being aware that President Yoon Suk-yeol intended to deploy the military to the 
National Assembly to overcome the confrontation with the National Assembly and that the 
Constitution grants the National Assembly the authority to request the lifting of martial law, 
Respondent nevertheless deployed police officers to the National Assembly and blocked 
members of the National Assembly from entering the National Assembly. This was done to 
carry out President Yoon Suk-yeol’s unconstitutional and unlawful instructions and thereby 
violated Article 77, Section (5) of the Constitution, representative democracy, and the 
principle of the separation of powers and infringed the constitutional authorities of members 
of the National Assembly, including their authority to deliberate and vote.
B. Assessment of the deployment of police to the Gwacheon headquarters of the National 
Election Commission and the Suwon Election Training Institute
Immediately after the declaration of the Martial Law, Yeo In-hyung, Commander of the 
Defense Counterintelligence Command, told Respondent by telephone that he would go to 
the Gwanak headquarters, the Gwacheon headquarters, and the Suwon Election Training 
Institute of the National Election Commission (hereinafter referred to as the “NEC”), and 
requested investigative personnel support. Respondent, at approximately 22:41 on December 
3, 2024, instructed Kim Junyoung, Commissioner General of the South Gyeonggi Provincial 
Police Agency, to control access to the NEC’s Gwacheon headquarters and the Suwon 
Election Training Institute. In response, police officers were deployed to those facilities to 
control access and were gradually withdrawn after the resolution demanding the lifting of the 
Martial Law was adopted.
Through the meeting at the safe house, Respondent knew that President Yoon Suk-yeol 
4intended to declare the Martial Law in order to overcome the confrontation with the National 
Assembly by mobilizing military forces and that the military would be deployed to the 
National Assembly and other locations. Through a telephone call with Yeo In-hyung, 
Commander of the Defense Counterintelligence Command, Respondent can also be regarded 
as having known that the military would be deployed to election commissions (hereinafter 
referred to as the “EC”) for investigative purposes. Moreover, it is clear that the 
circumstances cited by President Yoon Suk-yeol at the safe house meeting, including the 
confrontation with the National Assembly, could not justify the declaration of emergency 
martial law under the Constitution or by the nature of the State’s emergency powers. 
Nevertheless, although Respondent had substantial reason to doubt the lawfulness of the 
process by which the military was to be deployed to the EC for investigative purposes 
pursuant to the Martial Law, Respondent deployed police officers to the NEC headquarters, 
which were the institutions to which the martial-law forces would be dispatched, and ordered 
that access be controlled. In the meantime, soldiers belonging to the Defense Intelligence 
Command conducted a warrantless search and seizure of the NEC’s Gwacheon headquarters, 
and soldiers belonging to the Army Special Warfare Command likewise entered those 
premises with the cooperation of the police.
Such conduct by Respondent supported the military that entered the EC’s premises 
pursuant to the unconstitutional and unlawful Martial Law, interfered with the EC’s 
performance of its duties and exercise of its authority, and thereby infringed the independence 
of the EC.
C. Assessment of the alleged incitement of riots and restriction of assembly at the “2024 
National Workers’ Rally in Commemoration of the Spirit of Jeon Tae-il”
It is acknowledged that, at the “2024 National Workers’ Rally in Commemoration of the 
Spirit of Jeon Tae-il” held on November 9, 2024, there were physical clashes between the 
police and the rally participants, that some participants were arrested in flagrante delicto , and 
that dissolution procedures for the rally were carried out. However, these acknowledged facts 
alone are insufficient to find that Respondent was involved in issuing a dissolution order for 
the rally or in the arrest of the participants, or that Respondent induced clashes between the 
police and the participants with the intent to create conditions justifying the declaration of the 
5Martial Law, and there is no other evidence to support such a finding.
2. Whether to remove Respondent from office
A. Article 7, Section (1) of the Constitution defines public officials as “servants of the 
entire people,” thereby proclaiming their duty to realize the public interest, and Article 5 of 
the Act on the Organization and Operation of National Police and Autonomous Police 
(hereinafter referred to as the “Police Act”), giving concrete expression to this principle, 
imposes on all police officers, including the Commissioner General of the National Police 
Agency, the duty to comply with the Constitution and statutes and to observe impartiality and 
neutrality. In addition, the Police Act and the National Assembly Act establish institutional 
safeguards to enable the Commissioner General of the National Police Agency to perform his 
duties with impartiality and neutrality. In light of these provisions, the Commissioner General 
of the National Police Agency is not merely in a position to execute the instructions of the 
President or other authorities without question, but bears the responsibility to ensure that the 
police under his command and supervision perform their duties in a manner that protects the 
freedom and rights of the people in accordance with the Constitution and statutes.
Nevertheless, after President Yoon Suk-yeol entirely prohibited the activities of the 
National Assembly by proclaiming the Martial Law and issuing the Proclamation, 
Respondent deployed police to the National Assembly and controlled access thereto, thereby 
interfering with the exercise of the National Assembly’s authority, which resulted in a delay 
in the adoption of the resolution demanding the lifting of the Martial Law. Respondent also 
supported the execution of the martial-law forces’ mission by deploying police to the EC 
headquarters and restricting access thereto. Such conduct by Respondent in and of itself 
constitutes a grave violation of representative democracy and the principle of separation of 
powers, and its negative impact on the constitutional order is also serious.
Respondent argues that, at the time of the Martial Law, there were insufficient grounds to 
regard it as unconstitutional or unlawful, and that Respondent therefore had no choice but to 
follow the instructions of President Yoon Suk-yeol. However, considering that Respondent, 
as a high-ranking public official with more than thirty years of service in the police, serves as 
the Commissioner General of the National Police Agency and therefore knows better than 
anyone else the duties and limits of the police as conferred by the Constitution and statutes; 
6that the circumstances cited by President Yoon Suk-yeol as grounds for the Martial Law, 
including the activities of the National Assembly, were clearly incapable in and of themselves 
of constituting justifiable grounds for the declaration of emergency martial law; that, 
immediately after the Martial Law was declared, members of the National Assembly and 
citizens gathered at the National Assembly to resist, and the military and police dispatched to 
the scene performed their duties in a passive manner, demonstrating that even ordinary 
members of society could readily recognize the unconstitutionality and unlawfulness of the 
Martial Law; and that, in the course of directly receiving instructions or requests related to 
the Martial Law from President Yoon Suk-yeol and others, Respondent appears to have 
understood President Yoon Suk-yeol’s intent in declaring the Martial Law, Respondent’s 
above argument cannot be accepted.
Moreover, Respondent’s above argument squarely contradicts the purpose of Article 7, 
Section (1) of the Constitution and Article 5 of the Police Act, which impose on the 
Commissioner General of the National Police Agency the duty, as a servant of the entire 
people, to comply with the Constitution and statutes and to observe impartiality and neutrality. 
Given that the exercise of the State’s emergency powers, such as the declaration of martial 
law, entails a serious risk of infringing constitutional values and the fundamental rights of the 
people, the Commissioner General of the National Police Agency, when receiving 
instructions from the President, must assess whether such instructions are constitutional and 
lawful in light of his own position and authority. Respondent’s assertion that he failed to 
recognize the unconstitutionality and unlawfulness of the Martial Law therefore amounts to 
no more than an admission that he failed to discharge his duties as Commissioner General of 
the National Police Agency.
B. The Commissioner General of the National Police Agency oversees national police 
affairs, administers the operations of the National Police Agency responsible for public 
security, and commands and supervises more than 130,000 police officers, other public 
officials of the Agency, and the heads of police agencies at all levels. In light of this, it is 
difficult to regard the national loss that would result from the removal of Respondent from 
office as insignificant. Nevertheless, although Respondent knew of the unconstitutionality 
and unlawfulness of the Martial Law and the Proclamation, Respondent instead mobilized the 
police under his command to confront members of the public, thereby causing the entire 
7police organization to lose the trust of the citizens.
Such violations of the law by Respondent are sufficiently serious to warrant depriving 
Respondent of the people’s trust indirectly vested in him through the President as the 
appointing authority. Therefore, the benefits of protecting the Constitution by removing 
Respondent from office overwhelmingly outweigh the national loss that would result from 
such removal.
Accordingly, Respondent is removed from the office of Commissioner General of the 
National Police Agency.
* This translation is provisional and subject to revision.
